title: Spotify to build 'artist-first AI music products' with major labels and indies - Music Ally
text:
Remember
those recent reports
about two of the three major labels being in talks with several companies about AI-music deals? And how one of them was Spotify but it was still “in the early stages… and no deal was imminent”?
Well, there’s been a development. This afternoon Spotify has announced plans to build “artist-first AI music products” with partners including all three major labels – Universal Music Group, Sony Music Group and Warner Music Group – as well as indies licensing agency Merlin and Believe.
In its blog post announcing the news
, Spotify described its intention to develop “responsible AI products that empower the artists and songwriters they represent, and connect them with the fans who support them” – while adding that it hopes to include more rightsholders and distributors in this process over time.
Here’s an important point: this is NOT a licensing announcement. Spotify has not yet licensed the catalogues of these partners to train the AI models for its new products. Today is more about it announcing its
intention
to license in the way that the rightsholders want: upfront.
“We’re working closely with our partners to ensure everything is properly licensed with the music industry upfront, ensuring everyone is compensated for uses of their work,” Spotify’s spokesperson told Music Ally.
Spotify’s blog post went into more detail about the ambitions.
“Some voices in the tech industry believe copyright should be abolished. We don’t. Musicians’ rights matter. Copyright is essential. If the music industry doesn’t lead in this moment, AI-powered innovation will happen elsewhere, without rights, consent, or compensation,” claimed Spotify.
“Together with rightsholders, artists, and songwriters, we are making significant investments in AI research and product development.”
“Artists and rightsholders will choose if and how to participate…”
Spotify
The announcement came with some promises. Spotify said that it will only develop these new AI products “through upfront agreements, not by asking for forgiveness later”, and that it will offer “choice in participation… artists and rightsholders will choose if and how to participate to ensure the use of AI tools aligns with the values of the people behind the music”.
Spotify also said that its AI products will “create wholly new revenue streams” for rightsholders and musicians “ensuring they are properly compensated for uses of their work and transparently credited for their contributions”
What are those products going to be, though? That’s where the fourth promise came in. “AI tools we develop will not replace human artistry. They will give artists new ways to be creative and connect with fans,” claimed Spotify.
So, a Suno-style ‘create your own songs’ tool does not appear to be the plan. “We will leverage our role as the place where more than 700 million people already come to listen to music every month to ensure that generative AI deepens artist-fan connections…”
Spotify said in the blog post that it has already begun work on the first products from these partnerships, and that it has also started building “a state-of-the-art generative AI research lab and product team” to work on them – “all shaped in close consultation with artists, producers, and songwriters”.
“Direct licensing in advance of launching new products is the only appropriate way. to build them”
Rob Stringer, Sony music
Note, this isn’t Spotify’s first AI research lab rodeo. In 2017 it hired
François Pachet
, one of the key experts in AI music, to lead its ‘Creator Technology Research Lab’ and work on (
as his website puts it
) “the next generation of AI-based tools for musicians”.
However, Pachet is not in charge of the new lab announced today. He left Spotify in June 2024, and has since co-founded two new startups:
Imagine All The People
(which explores AI’s use for marketing) and Ynosound (which focuses on AI-assisted music creation).
“Our company brings deep research expertise to this opportunity and we’re actively growing our AI team and capabilities to drive the continued growth of the entire music ecosystem,” said Spotify’s co-president and chief product and technology officer (and
soon to be co-CEO
) Gustav Söderström in a statement.
Spotify’s announcement is a big deal, certainly for the major labels, with each of their bosses providing a statement to accompany the news.
UMG chief Sir Lucian Grainge said that “it is essential that we work with strategic partners such as Spotify to enable Gen AI products within a thriving commercial landscape in which artists, songwriters, fans, music companies and technology companies can all flourish”.
Meanwhile, Sony Music boss Rob Stringer said the partnership is “an acknowledgement that direct licensing in advance of launching new products is the only appropriate way to build them and demonstrates how a properly functioning market benefits everyone in the ecosystem and fuels innovation”.
WMG head Robert Kyncl praised “partners who understand the necessity for new AI licensing deals that protect and compensate rightsholders and the creative community”, adding that “we support Spotify’s thoughtful AI guardrails, and welcome the opportunity to pioneer the future together.”
There was also praise from Merlin COO Charlie Lexton, who said that Spotify’s plans “respect and value copyright, respect and value artists and want to enrich, not displace, the creative community”, and Believe CEO Denis Ladegaillerie, who described the plans as “value-creative AI tools that will fuel artist development and unlock new creative and commercial opportunities”.
“A firm statement that independent music will not be invited late to the AI party”
Today’s news follows
Spotify’s recent partnership with ChatGPT
for an integration into the latter service turning it into a tool for music discovery and listening. Spotify also recently
announced measures to tackle the negative side of AI music
: spam tracks, deepfakes and a lack of transparency over how and when GenAI has been used to make tracks.
Spotify is not the first big global music service to work with major labels on new, experimental AI-music products. In August 2023,
YouTube launched its ‘Music AI Incubator’ with UMG
as its first partner.
The plan for that was to test new products and tools with a panel of artists and producers before they launched commercially. Later that year its first experiment, ‘
Dream Track in YouTube Shorts
‘, went live with nine artists making AI voice clones available for a few Shorts creators to play with in their videos.
So, YouTube got in first, but Spotify’s announcement today is the biggest such partnership we’ve seen, particularly the fact that all three majors plus Merlin and Believe are on board from day one. The inclusion of the latter two companies is important, too: a firm statement that independent music will not be invited late to the AI party.
All of the partners are happy with Spotify’s plans and attitude, but now the announcement is out, we’ll find out what artists and songwriters make of it.
It’s fair to say that that musicians who already distrust Spotify for whatever reason – streaming-economy debates, CEO arms investments, misinfo-Rogan etc – won’t welcome today’s news.
If an artist or songwriter already sees Spotify as a company that doesn’t have their best interests at heart, that will carry through into how they view its AI promises.
For other musicians, the distrust may lie more on the rightsholder side of these partnerships. As and when Spotify does strike licensing deals for catalogues, artists and songwriters will want to know the details – and whether (as suggested in Spotify’s blog post) they can choose not to participate if they don’t want to.
Still, from an industry perspective, today’s announcement is positive news, and an approach that rightsholders have been arguing for: license first, then build – not the other way around.
It’s certainly a stark contrast to what’s happening in the TV and film world with OpenAI’s Sora 2 app and model, where the company launched a product, sparked
a tidal wave of AI-made clips featuring copyrighted characters
, and
then had to backtrack
– while watching talent agencies
opt their talent out of the platform en masse
.
“Video hits people, particularly rights owners, very differently than still images it turns out,”
wrote possibly-penitent-for-at-least-a-few-minutes OpenAI CEO Sam Altman
.
So would music. With that in mind, Spotify’s approach is very welcome: it’s one that could pull the wider GenAI music market in the direction of the kind of partnerships and licensing that the industry has been calling so loudly for.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
